text.fullText: February 17. 2026 VIA EMAIL Lauren Kasparek Oceans, Wetlands, and Communities Division Office of Water (4502-T) Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 Submitted under Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 via www.regulations.gov RE: Minnesota Pollution Control Agency Comments Regarding the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s Proposed Rule,” Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations,” published in the Federal Register January 15, 2026. Dear Lauren Kasparek: The Minnesota Pollution Control Agency (MPCA) appreciates the opportunity to provide comment on the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) Proposed 401 Water Quality Certification rule, Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929. As the delegated authority for administering Clean Water Act (CWA) programs (including development and implementation of Water Quality Standards and the State’s Section 401 Water Quality Certification program), the MPCA is responsible for protecting the quality of Minnesota’s waters. Minnesota has more than 10,000 lakes, tens of thousands of miles of streams and rivers, and over ten million acres of wetlands. All of these waters are protected by Minnesota’s rigorous water quality standards, and all serve a vital purpose in ensuring the overall health of Minnesota’s people and ecosystems. The MPCA recognizes the responsibility to review long-standing environmental regulations to ensure they continue to reflect the most recent and well-supported legal and scientific precepts. We are concerned EPA is considering changes to the cooperative federalism required by the CWA to Section 401 rules. At a high level, MPCA’s comments relate to: support for maintaining the cooperative federalism origin and purpose of Section 401; support for continued state and federal negotiations on Reasonable Periods of Time to account for specific project needs and public transparency; and support for considering full project impacts to water quality in 401 certification reviews. Support for Cooperative Federalism within 401 The CWA is built upon a foundation of cooperative federalism wherein states/Tribes are granted broad authority to implement and enforce many CWA programs in a manner that is specific to the needs and laws of that state/Tribe. Indeed, Section 101 of the CWA specifically identifies in subpart (b) “It is the policy of the Congress to recognize, preserve, and protect the primary responsibilities and rights of States to prevent, reduce, and eliminate pollution, to plan the development and use (including restoration, preservation, and enhancement) of land and water resources, and to consult with the Administrator in the exercise of his authority under this chapter.” In providing states/Tribes the Lauren Kasparek Page 2 February 17, 2026 authority to set water quality standards, EPA clearly recognized States and Tribes as the authority on those standards, from the beneficial uses identified for specific waters to the conditions that support and ensure attainment and maintenance of those uses. Section 401 provides a bridge between state/Tribal water quality standards and the federal permitting/licensing program. Where a state/Tribe has full authority through permitting, it would be able to fully ensure that its own permitting decisions comport with water quality standards and other relevant provisions of state/Tribal law and policy. Where permitting and licensing duties instead fall to a federal agency (and in most cases, a federal agency not entrusted with water quality authorities/expertise), the 401 Certification provides states/Tribes the opportunity to ensure federal decisions comply with state/Tribal water quality requirements. The MPCA is concerned about any rule changes that limit the role of states as independent water regulators who must review projects to ensure compliance with state water quality standards. In particular, states and Tribes should be able to set their own requirements for submittals. Setting up reasonable periods of time based on nation-wide criteria means states will be forced to work with incomplete information or deny projects if applicants are not responsive to requests. It will create delays and confusion if states cannot set application contents up in advance for applicants. Support for Continued State and Federal Coordination on Reasonable Periods of Time The MPCA supports efficiency and certainty in regulatory programs while maintaining appropriate public notice and technical review of projects. The 2023 rule struck a balance of encouraging State and Federal authorities to coordinate on establishing Reasonable Periods of Time while identifying six months as a deadline if no agreement can be reached. MPCA reviews projects that range in complexity and controversy. Some projects can be reviewed and certified in a matter of weeks while others require significant technical and public review. The MPCA has also committed to pre-notice review of decisions for Tribal nations within Minnesota that have shared water resources or treaty resources. Although many project decisions can be issued earlier, any rule changes should not unilaterally limit the Reasonable Period of Time. It is up to States and Tribes to review and notice projects and determine adequate timelines for doing so within the constraints of CWA deadlines. The proposed rule also limits 401(a)(2) timelines to 90 days which does not respect Tribal sovereignty or acknowledge the complex water quality impacts of large projects like mines and pipelines. Support for Consideration of Full Project Impacts in 401 Certifications States and Tribes have built and implemented 401 Certification programs around nearly fifty years of consistent regulatory and statutory interpretation, wherein they could fully consider and address the state or Tribal water quality implications of federally permitted/licensed projects. The sudden and drastic narrowing of that scope in EPA’s 2020 rule had significant impacts on state and Tribal regulatory programs and, more importantly, water quality across the nation. The 2023 401 rule affirmed the longstanding interpretation (and implementation) of the scope needed to protect state/Tribal water quality. EPA should ensure that the scope of 401 Certification allows states/Tribes to evaluate and address all water quality impacts, including those to state-/Tribal-only waters. The proposed 2026 rule ignores decades of law and practice where states and Tribes evaluated the impacts of the project and its compliance with their own water quality standards. The MPCA does not support the rule as proposed because it undercuts our role as co-regulators of our waters. We encourage you to withdraw this rulemaking and maintain the 2023 401 rule or develop a rule that respects states’ role as co-regulators and achieves water quality benefits. Minnesota is Lauren Kasparek Page 3 February 17, 2026 committed to working with EPA and other federal partners to protect and maintain the high quality of our extraordinary water resources. We appreciate your consideration of our comments. If you have any questions regarding this letter, please contact Kirsten Barta (651-757-2827, or kirsten.barta@state.mn.us) of my staff. Sincerely, Brian Timerson This document has been electronically signed. Brian Timerson Manager Resource Management and Assistance Division BT/KB:kj
